video_title: Anthropic's Fable Backlash, Nationalizing AI, Inflation Heats Up & California’s Broken Elections
gist: The All-In hosts argue Anthropic's new Fable 5 model, with mandatory 30-day prompt retention and silent downgrading of users doing AI/bio/chip research, is regulatory-capture-driven censorship that will push enterprises toward Chinese open-source models. They debate Bernie Sanders' plan to seize 50% of AI-company equity, a hotter-than-expected May CPI/PPI print tied to the Iran war, and allege California's mail-in/ballot-harvesting laws have turned elections into appointments.

themes.theme: Anthropic Fable 5: censorship, surveillance and enterprise risk

insights.claim: Anthropic's new Fable 5 model tops nearly every benchmark but its tokens cost roughly twice as much as Opus 4.8, with Anthropic arguing total spend stays lower because the model is more capable per token.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Higher per-token pricing on frontier models reframes the AI cost debate around efficiency-per-task rather than raw token price; watch whether Anthropic's pricing power holds as cheaper open-source alternatives improve.
insights.quote: The tokens, however, cost twice as much as Opus 4.8, but it should use less tokens overall because it's theoretically that much better.

insights.claim: Anthropic retains every prompt, output and shared context for at least 30 days on Fable/Mythos-class models with no exceptions, overriding even enterprise zero-data-retention agreements, in order to build user profiles and gate capabilities.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Mandatory retention voids prior ZDR contracts, creating compliance and IP-leakage risk that pushes regulated enterprises toward self-hosted models; a concrete catalyst for the open-source migration thesis.
insights.quote: they are now retaining for 30 days every prompt and every output you send to one of these mythos class models. There are no exceptions.

insights.claim: Anthropic silently downgrades users it suspects of frontier AI, machine-learning or chip-design research to a lesser model, rewriting prompts and still charging full price, which the hosts call overtly anti-competitive.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: If a dominant lab degrades output for competitors' R&D use, it raises antitrust exposure and gives rivals (including open-source and Chinese labs) a marketing wedge for reliability and neutrality.
insights.quote: they would even do things like rewrite your prompt in the background so they would give you a nerfed answer they would not tell you what they were doing they still charge you for the product

insights.claim: Anthropic's safeguards flag and downgrade benign queries, with reported examples including a basic mitochondria question and Ben Thompson being cut off asking about cancer risk and GLP-1 drugs.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Overbroad false positives degrade the product for legitimate scientific and medical users, accelerating churn to alternatives; a usability red flag investors should weigh against benchmark wins.
insights.quote: when they simply just asked a question about mitochondria, they were basically downgraded

insights.claim: Friedberg says Anthropic and peers have over the last few weeks restricted his genomics work (RNA guide design, genetic-construct design, phenotype prediction), forcing his company to switch to locally run open-source models.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Real-world enterprise defection from closed labs to self-hosted models is a leading indicator that frontier-lab safety restrictions are destroying paid demand in high-value verticals like biotech.
insights.quote: Over the last couple of weeks, they've begun to restrict the ability to use the models to do that work.

insights.claim: Chamath argues no company can safely build on Anthropic because a downstream scientist, executive or researcher could unknowingly trip a filter and get cut off, making single-vendor AI a non-starter that demands diversified, self-governed model access.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Single-point-of-failure risk is becoming a board-level underwriting question, favoring multi-model orchestration platforms and open-source/self-hosted deployments.
insights.quote: If you're a company, I think it's almost a non-starter. And the reason is because you could accidentally trip one of these things without even knowing it.

insights.claim: Chamath warns Anthropic could steer information toward partners it has commercial deals with, e.g. favoring a Novartis GLP-1 over Lilly's or JP Morgan over Citi, without users ever knowing the answer was shaped.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Undisclosed commercial bias in model outputs is a latent legal and trust liability; enterprises and regulators may demand auditable model-run traces, a potential new compliance market.
insights.quote: if Novartis has a competitive GLP1 drug to Lily and they have a strategic deal with Novartis and not one with Lily, now there's an impetus to shape how people get information.

insights.claim: Sacks contends Anthropic's lack of any real KYC is the tell that its true goal is regulatory capture, since a genuinely safety-motivated lab could implement identity verification and security bonds to let vetted users like Friedberg access uncensored models.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: If safety theater masks an anti-open-source agenda, the policy fight is really about market structure; watch for KYC adoption as a credibility signal versus continued blanket restrictions.
insights.quote: Anthropic could implement KYC tomorrow, but it does not.

insights.claim: Sacks says Dario Amodei published a blog post arguing transparency is no longer enough and calling for a new FAA- or FDA-style agency to approve all AI models, aimed at restricting open-source competitors that cannot be regulated.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: A model-approval regulator would entrench incumbents and kneecap open-source, reshaping the competitive landscape toward a closed duopoly; a key policy risk for AI investing.
insights.quote: saying that transparency was no longer good enough that we needed to have a new regulatory agency like an FAA or maybe an FDA to approve all models

insights.claim: The hosts assert the best open-source AI models today are Chinese, so US lab restrictions are actively pushing American startups and enterprises onto Chinese models and handing China an advantage in biotech, materials science and industrial systems.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Self-imposed US restrictions as a tailwind for Chinese open-source adoption is a geopolitical and competitive thesis; watch enterprise adoption data and US open-source funding responses.
insights.quote: the the American open source models are not as good as the Chinese open source models. So the restrictions that Anthropic and others are putting upon themselves and upon the industry is forcing a lot of companies to go and get open source Chinese models

insights.claim: Friedberg's company uses the Arc Institute's open-source genome language model (funded by the Collisons) to score whether DNA variants are beneficial, illustrating philanthropically funded open models displacing closed proprietary tools.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Open-source frontier models in specialized domains are already production-grade and freely copied, undermining the moat of closed labs in those verticals.
insights.quote: the Ark Institute ingested all the world's genomic data that they could get access to and it's it's a genome language model basically

insights.claim: Friedberg argues AI cannot be turned off because open-sourced models are already fully published like a printed book, so any attempt to regulate access merely advantages those who ignore the rules.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: If frontier capability is already irreversibly distributed, regulation can only shift competitive advantage, not contain capability; supports betting on open-source proliferation over containment.
insights.quote: Once the book has been printed, anyone can use their own Xerox machine at home to make copies of it

insights.claim: The major AI labs and DNA/RNA synthesis CEOs signed a letter to codify mandatory nucleic-acid synthesis screening, building on the voluntary 2009 International Gene Synthesis Consortium, as a model for output-level rather than access-level safeguards.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Output-stage screening (at DNA manufacture) is a politically viable safeguard that avoids gatekeeping model access, pointing to where workable AI-bio regulation may actually land.
insights.quote: it's called in support of mandatory nucleic acid synthetic screening and recordkeeping

insights.claim: Friedberg predicts companies with proprietary data will fine-tune open-source base models into their own domain models (e.g. a genome language model), reducing dependence on closed frontier labs.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: The next enterprise move is owning fine-tuned proprietary models, shrinking closed-lab API revenue in data-rich verticals; a structural headwind for usage-based AI revenue.
insights.quote: we'll start with the core model, combine it with our data, and then we'll have our own genome language model

themes.theme: Nationalizing AI: Sanders' equity seizure and sovereign wealth funds

insights.claim: Bernie Sanders' proposed American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act would impose a one-time 50% tax on the stock (not profits) of the largest AI companies including OpenAI, Anthropic and xAI, placing shares in a government fund with public voting rights and board seats.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A 50% equity confiscation proposal, however unlikely to pass, signals rising political risk to AI-company valuations and a horseshoe coalition spanning Bernie, Bannon and Trump.
insights.quote: a one-time 50% tax on stock, not profits of the largest AI companies, including Open AI anthropic and XAI

insights.claim: Sacks opposes Sanders' plan as straight confiscation but says he could support voluntary public participation because AI CEOs themselves keep telling the public they will cause 50% job loss while gatekeeping the output trained on humanity's free data.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: AI labs' own doom messaging is manufacturing political demand for wealth redistribution; the messaging itself is a self-inflicted valuation risk.
insights.quote: You've got all these AI CEOs telling the public that they're going to basically put half of them out of work.

insights.claim: Friedberg proposes restructuring Social Security from a defined-benefit system (whose trust fund holds a single ~$4 trillion Treasury certificate) into individual accounts allowed to own equities, including AI companies, as a US sovereign wealth fund.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: A Social-Security-to-equities reform along Canadian/Australian superannuation lines would create a massive new structural equity bid; a recurring policy idea worth tracking for market implications.
insights.quote: The US government owes the Social Security trust fund $4 trillion. That's all it is. It's a bond. They're only allowed to own treasuries. We should revamp that.

insights.claim: Chamath argues AI's marginal cost per user is high (every user taxes a GPU, electrons and memory), unlike the internet's near-zero marginal cost, which gives the government a strong leverage case to demand equity in exchange for critical power and infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: High marginal cost means AI is a fixed-plus-variable business reliant on state-enabled power and infrastructure, weakening the zero-marginal-cost monopoly economics that minted internet giants and inviting equity claims.
insights.quote: The incremental cost of performing AI is excessive and large. That contrasts and compares to the incremental cost before AI which was zero.

insights.claim: Chamath says a gigawatt of data-center capacity now costs about $100 billion, up roughly 20x from the $4-5 billion when he started his Arizona project, making his planned 2-3 gigawatts cost up to $300 billion and putting open-source compute out of reach for private players.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: A 20x escalation in gigawatt buildout cost is a massive capital moat that concentrates frontier AI among hyperscalers and the state, structurally limiting open-source compute access.
insights.quote: a gigawatt now costs a hundred billion dollars guys

insights.claim: Chamath reveals he has 2,000 zoned-and-approved acres in Arizona permitted for a 2-gigawatt data center plus an offer on another gigawatt, and is leaning toward building rather than flipping to Blackstone/Brookfield/Google specifically to guarantee open-source model access.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: An insider deliberately retaining (not flipping) gigawatt-scale compute to support open-source signals both the strategic value of power assets and the perceived threat to open-source access.
insights.quote: Two years ago, I bought 2,000 acres in Arizona with a partner. We got it zoned. We got it approved. So, we're allowed to build a 2 gawatt data center.

insights.claim: Friedberg insists AI is net job-creating because its leverage is overwhelmingly on the revenue side (one engineer doing 100x output, more products) rather than cost-cutting, citing his own team requesting 15 additional engineering headcount.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: If the AI-job-loss narrative is wrong and productivity drives hiring, it undercuts both the political case for equity seizure and bearish labor-market positioning; watch jobs and productivity data.
insights.quote: The real opportunity with AI is on the revenue side where suddenly one engineer can do a hundred times or a thousand times what they used to be able to do.

insights.claim: Sacks cites a blowout May jobs report of 172,000 new jobs (over twice expectations), 4.3% unemployment, hundreds of thousands of new construction jobs and software-developer jobs at a three-year high to argue the AI job-loss apocalypse is not in the data.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Strong May labor data contradicts AI-displacement fears and supports a soft-landing/growth view, relevant to rate and equity positioning.
insights.quote: We just had a blowout jobs report in May. 172,000 new jobs, over twice what economists were expecting.

insights.claim: The hosts distinguish the AI CEOs' divergent job claims: Dario Amodei predicted 50% entry-level knowledge-worker job loss within 1-5 years and is doubling down, while Sam Altman has walked back his job-loss prediction citing the data and Elon Musk frames a utopian post-work abundance with universal high income.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Differentiating the CEOs' positions matters for reading policy signals; Altman's pragmatic retreat versus Amodei's doom stance shapes which firm faces more regulatory/seizure risk.
insights.quote: Dario said specifically 50% job loss for entry-level knowledge workers in the next 1 to 5 years and he said that one year ago

insights.claim: Sacks notes OpenAI and Anthropic are public benefit corporations whose boards have a dual mandate to balance shareholders against a stated public mission, which Sacks frames as weakening their defense against partial nationalization.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: PBC structure could be leveraged politically to justify public claims on equity, a structural vulnerability unique to these labs versus normal C-corps.
insights.quote: open AI and anthropic are quote unquote public benefit corporations

insights.claim: Chamath says if he ran a US sovereign wealth fund he would negotiate for around 75% of the major AI companies, framing partial nationalization as a negotiation over critical infrastructure rather than confiscation.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: An influential investor openly contemplating majority state ownership of AI labs underscores how mainstream the nationalization conversation has become; a tail risk for AI equity holders.
insights.quote: if it were me running the sovereign wealth fund, yeah, I'd probably own 75% of these companies when I was done

insights.claim: Polymarket odds cited put SpaceX at 100%, Anthropic at 83% and OpenAI at 48% chance of going public before 2027, with OpenAI having disclosed it privately filed.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Prediction-market IPO odds and OpenAI's disclosed private filing are concrete liquidity-event signals for late-stage AI investors.
insights.quote: SpaceX 100% anthropic 83% chance opening 48% chance they uh since our last episode disclosed that they have privately filed

themes.theme: Venture math: power-law of giant outcomes

insights.claim: Citing Thomas Laffont, the hosts note that in this cycle a $100B-valued company is more likely to 10x than a $10B company is, with odds of a unicorn reaching decacorn at 8%, decacorn to centicorn 13%, and centicorn to a $1 trillion market cap 31%.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: An inverted power law where conditional 10x odds rise with scale argues for concentrating capital in the largest private winners rather than diversifying small; a direct allocation signal.
insights.quote: odds of a unicorn getting to decacorn was 8%. The odds of a decacorn getting to a senicorn was about double that was 13%.

insights.claim: Chamath speculates roughly 60% of trillion-dollar-market-cap companies could reach $10 trillion in the next few years, extrapolating the rising-odds power-law trend.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: If conditional 10x odds keep doubling at higher tiers, the largest mega-caps may remain the highest-expected-return holdings; an exploratory but actionable framing for concentration.
insights.quote: trillion dollar market cap companies getting to 10 trillion in the next few years

themes.theme: Inflation, oil and the Iran war

insights.claim: May CPI came in at 4.2% year-over-year (highest since April 2023) and PPI at 6.5% year-over-year (highest since late 2022), with Polymarket pricing a 21% chance inflation hits 5% in 2026 and a 49% chance of a Fed hike this year, up from under 10% before the Iran war.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A reaccelerating inflation print with a jump in Fed-hike odds is a direct macro signal for rates and duration positioning; the Iran war is the swing variable.
insights.quote: CPI came in at 4.2% year-over-year, highest since April 2023. There's your chart. PPI came in at 6.5% year-over-year, highest since the end of 2022.

insights.claim: Friedberg predicts overnight rates could move north of 5.5-6% under a Kevin Warsh Fed, driven by an Iran-war energy blip plus structural government overspending and rising rates as fiscal conditions unravel.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: A call for 5.5-6% overnight rates is materially above current pricing and bearish for long-duration and rate-sensitive tech; watch Warsh appointment signals and oil.
insights.quote: I think we could see north of five and a half, 6% overnight rates. It's not unforeseeable.

insights.claim: Chamath argues China has capped global oil near sub-$100 by smoothing energy consumption and pulling forward solar, but if China exhausts reserves and must buy ~3 million extra barrels/day on the spot market, oil could spike to $150-200, transmitting broadly into CPI via input costs worldwide.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Chinese reserve depletion is the key tail risk for an oil-driven inflation spike; the PPI print is flagged as an early alarm to off-ramp the Iran war.
insights.quote: there's a very big risk that oil gets into the well past 100 and maybe between 150 and 200 a barrel

insights.claim: Sacks notes the PPI print was largely in line with expectations, which is why the market rose (NASDAQ up ~2.5%) rather than selling off, implying the market is pricing in a near-term resolution to the Iran war.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: An up-market on a hot-but-expected print shows positioning is leaning toward de-escalation; a war resolution or escalation becomes the dominant near-term equity catalyst.
insights.quote: NASDAQ up 2 and a half% today. That normally doesn't happen when there's a hot print

insights.claim: Chamath frames excess government spending as the single root cause of US wealth inequality and inflation, with late-stage higher rates as the inevitable manifestation.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: A structural overspending-drives-rates thesis argues for a persistently higher-rate regime regardless of cyclical prints; relevant to long-term bond and equity duration views.
insights.quote: The core of the network is overspending by the government

themes.theme: California elections: ballot harvesting and 'legal fraud'

insights.claim: In the LA mayoral primary, election-day in-person voting favored Spencer Pratt (35% vs Karen Bass 29%, Nithya Raman 26%), but late-arriving mail-in ballots flipped the result, with Pratt's share falling by a third while Raman's rose ~80%, concentrated around Skid Row.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: The hosts treat the in-person vs late mail-in divergence as evidence the system enables manipulation; relevant to political-risk reads on California governance and electoral legitimacy debates.
insights.quote: Pratt's post election mailin ballots declined by one/ird. So statistically the population of people that send in their ballots late reduced for Prat

insights.claim: Friedberg argues California laws (AB 1921 legalizing unlimited ballot harvesting, mandatory mailing of ballots to all registered voters, and registration without citizenship proof or ID) have turned elections into 'appointments' that are legal but exploitable via adverse selection.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Framing electoral outcomes as a function of legal-but-exploitable rules underpins a thesis of single-party entrenchment in California, a structural backdrop for state policy and migration risk.
insights.quote: California Assembly Bill 1921 legalized the practice of unlimited ballot harvesting in the state.

insights.claim: Sacks claims California mails ~23-24 million ballots while only ~9.5 million people vote, leaving ~14 million loose ballots amid dirty voter rolls, ~40% signature-match thresholds, anonymous witness signatures and no chain of custody, which he calls a system designed for fraud.
insights.speaker: David Sacks
insights.so_what: Allegations of mass surplus ballots and weak verification fuel the political fight over voter-ID ballot measures; outcomes shape California's governance trajectory and one-party dynamics.
insights.quote: California sends out ballots to every registered voters. It's something like 23 24 million people. Only 9 and a half million people vote. So, what happens to the other 14 million ballots?

insights.claim: Chamath characterizes California as a de facto one-party autocracy where machine-shaped laws make every office a one-way door, predicting the resulting governance rot will take decades to fully manifest in the state's decline.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: A multi-decade governance-decline thesis for California is relevant to long-horizon decisions on business location, real estate and state fiscal credit.
insights.quote: live in a autocracy in California where one political machine has shaped the laws so inexurably

insights.claim: Calacanis counters that despite chronic litigation and Heritage Foundation tracking, no major modern US election has been shown to be swung by fraud, and the appearance of impropriety, not proven outcome-changing fraud, is the real problem.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A skeptical counterpoint that fraud rarely swings major elections tempers the panel's strongest claims; useful for calibrating the political-narrative risk versus reality.
insights.quote: I don't think that there has been an election a major election that has been swung by election fraud
